data.title: AI Capex Should Cripple Next Profit Cycle For S&P 500
data.description:
Any time investing feels like it is easy and also a spectator sport, you have to ask yourself some important questions. Today, it does feel easy, unless you have actually grown up learning finance. For those who were fortunate (or unfortunate, depending on your perspective) to have learned the rules, this time appears surreal. It is as if investors are once again turning a blind eye to the math and saying, "No, this time two plus two does equal two hundred." We look at this equation from the perspective of AI and the capex being sunk in. We ask the questions that need to be asked.

How Much Is Too Much?

When you give big tech valuations that make no sense, they will spend on things that don't make sense. We have had cycles before where capex was high, but this is something else.

It is important to note that from an accounting perspective, capex is investing and stays off the books until the projects are actually put into use. Investors who are following just the profit statement are missing just how much is being tied to the future. Granted these stocks are ridiculously expensive to begin with, even on a pure earnings yield (inverse of P/E) basis, Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), Oracle Corp. (ORCL), and Microsoft Corp. (MSFT) are very expensive. The 3 sport earnings yields are 3.85%, 1.49%, and 2.62%, respectively. But the picture worsens dramatically when you look at free cash flow yields.

ORCL appears the most expensive of the bunch, and investors are on the hook to justify a negative free cash flow yield. This number is also not revealing the true impact, as it is a trailing 12-month number. The most recent quarter's capex was off the charts. Well, Y-charts did manage to fit it in, but you get the picture.

Based on this run-rate, ORCL now has a negative 3% free cash flow yield. Don't expect this boom to stop until the market reacts negatively to this massive spend.

ORCL will be raising debt left, right, and center, and the $18 billion sale was just a warm-up.

When Will Depreciation Start To Hit The Books?

Of course there are purists out there that will start labelling all these stocks as cheap; as for them, capex is just a down payment on a long runway of growth. But absolutely no one is taking into account just how big a move we will see in depreciation for all these stocks and what that will do to the income statement. ORCL is a good example, as despite revenues being up 12%, net income was flat, and the culprit was depreciation, which was 68% year-over-year.

This depreciation will accelerate to quadruple this run-rate over the next 5 years. This might sound far-fetched, but if we multiply the current depreciation by 4, you still come up short on the quarterly capex run-rate of $8.5 billion. Below we show just how quickly the situation has deteriorated.

The others are not this bad, but the burn rate is fairly intense.

How Will Tech Make Money When AI Is Unprofitable At Today's Prices?

Everyone knows that AI (LLMs, to be accurate) are loss-making ventures today. How investors are making the leap from that to big profits down the line boggles the imagination. This requires an ostrich approach, especially in light of what is happening to pricing.

The revenue concentration comes into sharp focus as OpenAI launched GPT-5 this week with dramatically lower pricing that could undercut Anthropic's premium positioning. Early comparisons show Claude Opus 4 costs roughly seven times more per million tokens than GPT-5 for certain tasks, creating immediate pressure on Anthropic's enterprise pricing strategy and potentially threatening its hard-won dominance in AI coding.

The pricing disparity signals a fundamental shift in competitive dynamics that will force enterprise procurement teams to reconsider vendor relationships built on performance rather than price. Companies managing exponentially growing AI budgets now face comparable capability at a fraction of the cost, creating unavoidable pressure in contract negotiations.

Source: VentureBeat

The current pricing on the US side of the pond looks like this.

Keep those in mind and add this news release from DeepSeek a few days back.

To sweeten the deal, DeepSeek also cut API pricing. Inputs now cost as little as $0.028 per cache hit, down from $0.07, while outputs dropped to $0.42 from $1.68. That pricing move underscores the startup's pitch: powerful models at a fraction of the cost of U.S. rivals.

DeepSeek has been turning heads with its lean training approach. Its reasoning-focused R1 model, for instance, cost just $294,000 to train using slightly more than 500 Nvidia (NVDA) H800 GPUs. By comparison, reports suggest Microsoft-backed OpenAI's GPT-4 burned through more than 10,000 GPUs.

Source: Yahoo Finance

For reference, OpenAI is expected to blow through $8-$14 billion in 2025 and 2026 while charging 25 times DeepSeek's output price.

The entire industry is in a deflationary revenue-per-user spiral while costs keep exploding.

Verdict

Reality is coming for this indiscriminate spending, and it will impact profits over many years. OpenAI may be the most immune, as it has not gotten investors even used to the idea of a positive income statement. Everything released from the company about the future of AI can be summed up in value in the chart below.

The rest of Big tech looks cheaper than the year 2000, but that is precisely because they are using their base business to finance this experiment. Ultimately there is nothing new here that will be able to sell outside of a slightly improved mousetrap for trillions of capex.

S&P 500 has become incredibly risky with extreme concentration risk.

Buyer Beware.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Borr Drilling: Mexico Momentum And Middle East Demand Drive Growth
data.description:
High Utilization, But Margin Pressures Persist

Borr Drilling Limited (NYSE:BORR) operates a fleet of shallow-water jack-up rigs for the offshore oil and gas producers. The company operates in the Middle East, Asia, and Latin America. Borr is positioned for steady growth, supported by high jack-up utilization and expanding international contracts. It aims to balance international expansion with strong liquidity. Following the recent policy upgrades, Mexico’s renewed push for production and private investment adds upside.

On the other hand, rig retirements tighten supply and limit oilfield services’ scale of operations. In this environment, BORR’s strong liquidity and disciplined spending are the key factors that will determine its ability to weather near-term volatility. Based on its long-duration work secured, I expect day rates and margins to remain resilient into 2026. Despite the stock’s relative overvaluation, a positive outlook and strong drivers validate my “Buy” call for the stock.

Industry Outlook

Year 2025 marks a volatile year for the energy sector as global conflicts, tariff uncertainty, and OPEC’s rollback of production cuts keep investors on the swivel. Brent briefly hit a high in June (25% up since May) as Middle East tensions raised supply concerns. But the momentum tapered off as OPEC production cuts reversed amidst a shaky global demand. It adversely affected day rates following the Saudi rig suspensions, although jack-up utilization stayed firm above 90%.

Despite the pressure, most of the idle rigs are being absorbed, with new demand in the Middle East expected to take up the rest by 2026–27. It appears that the frac activity is sliding. The frac spread count, according to Primary Vision, is already down 12% from Q2. In Mexico, some units are estimated to have been retired, and several others are being held for sale.

Company Outlook

Given the backdrop, Borr expects Q3 activity and performance to be similar to Q2, as the management’s EBITDA estimates are close to $470 million. Mexico’s renewed support for Pemex and its 1.8 million bpd target should improve liquidity and open more drilling opportunities. Private investment projects could also provide upside for Borr in Mexico.

Near-term volatility may persist, but long-term jack-up fundamentals look strong. Global energy demand is growing; shallow water projects remain attractive. With no new builds on the horizon and an aging fleet, I expect utilization to remain high and support steady day rates.

Mexico’s Significance And Other Projects

Mexico is becoming Borr’s key market. The country is boosting Pemex’s liquidity with large debt financings and reaffirming its production goal, which should support drilling activity. Borr is well placed to win incremental work, including private projects that are set to become a bigger part of Mexico’s output. At the same time, rig retirements are picking up, tightening supply and adding support to market fundamentals.

Borr added 14 new contracts in 2025, boosting backlog by $318 million and extending earnings visibility. Recent wins in Vietnam, the Middle East, and Mexico highlight strong utilization. The company has secured rigs with long-duration work that could lift day rates. Despite a suspension notice for one rig in June, the steady flow of new awards underlines Borr’s ability to limit idle time and strengthen its regional positions.

Risk Factors

Global instability, from the war in Ukraine to tensions in the Middle East and Asia, continues to create uncertainty for oil and gas markets. Industry capital spending has been volatile, with gradual cuts in capex over the past few years and a collapse in oil prices in 2020, followed by a recovery. But the price swings have been ongoing.

Also, higher oil prices don’t always translate into sustained drilling, as operators can still cancel or defer programs based on exploration results, capital access, or other priorities. Regional differences also matter, with higher-cost basins being slower to recover and rig relocations leading to tighter competition in matured markets.

My Estimates

Over the past 10 quarters, its adjusted EBITDA increased by 12% on average. I expect Borr’s EBITDA margin to edge higher or stay flat through mid-2026. Strong utilization and contract visibility should drive profitability, although the temporary slowdown in Mexico or higher rig maintenance costs can put the brake on it.

Over the next four quarters, I expect its adjusted EBITDA to increase by 11%–13%. Given BORR’s trailing-12-month adjusted EBITDA of $481 million, I expect its adjusted EBITDA to increase to ~$540 million in the next four quarters.

Analyzing Q2 Drivers

From Q1 to Q2 (as released in its Q2 2025 earnings dated August 13), BORR’s revenue rose by 24%, and EBITDA jumped 39%. Approximately 22 of its 24 rigs were active, pushing technical utilization at 99.6% in Q2.

As I discussed above, a multi-rig contract in Asia and a new contract for the Arabia II can bolster BORR’s fleet utilization. Contract coverage reached 84% for 2025, with solid day rates. More operating days for the Vale and Tor (modern jack-up rigs) improved day rate revenue in Q2. Other factors pushing the Q2 sales higher were new starts in Brazil and Mexico and higher management contract revenue from the Galar recommencement.

Cash Flows And Balance Sheet

Borr's cash flow from operations increased to $145 million in 6M 2025 from $40 million a year ago. Capex increased from $22 million to $39 million due to jack-up maintenance and rig activations. So, free cash flow also increased from $18 million a year ago to $107 million in 1H 2025. The rise in cash flows relates to an increase in the amount due to higher receivables from Pemex and other parties.

Upon diving deep into the receivable issue, we note that the company has $60 million to $65 million of outstanding payments from Mexico. Note that a rise in receivables adds to working capital and cash flow from operations but also increases the chance of non-receipt or delayed receipt. However, recent government financing efforts are expected to ease the situation. The company now expects payments against the receivable to start flowing from Q3. Also, the company’s accrued revenue has risen while rig rates have increased versus last quarter. As of June 30, 2025, BORR's liquidity (cash balance plus available credit facility) was $261 million.

Recently, Borr Drilling raised $102.5 million in equity and expanded its credit facilities. It boosted its liquidity by $200 million. The move aims to strengthen the balance sheet and support long-term strategic goals.

Relative Valuation And Target Price

BORR's forward EV/EBITDA multiple versus the current multiple is expected to expand versus an unchanged multiple for its peers. This usually results in a lower EV/EBITDA multiple compared to its peers. However, its EV/EBITDA multiple (5.8x) is higher than its peers' (PDS, SDRL, and PTEN) average of 5.1x. So, the stock is relatively overvalued.

BORR's five-year average EV/EBITDA multiple is 74x. I believe the industry dynamics have undergone a complete transformation, and the stock will not trade at a similar multiple anytime soon. If it trades at the peers' average, the stock price can decrease by 33%. This, also, seems unlikely because BORR’s growth outlook is more robust than its peers.

As I discussed earlier in the article, I expect 11%-13% adjusted EBITDA growth in the next four quarters. Feeding these values into the EV calculation and applying the current EV/EBITDA multiple of 5.8x, I think the stock should trade between $4.2 and $4.4, implying a 57% upside. Although the stock appears overvalued, I think the 29% fall over the past year has rendered it attractive to investors.

Wall Street Rating

Two sell-side analysts rated BORR a "buy" (including a "strong buy"), three analysts rated it a "hold," and none rated it a "sell." The consensus target price is $3.13, suggesting a 1% upside at the current price. Given the company's operational recovery, I think Wall Street analysts are underestimating the return potential.

What's The Take On BORR?

I see indications of an international drilling activity slowdown on the horizon. BORR’s jack-up utilization remains strong, but day rates are under pressure from Saudi drilling suspensions. Frac activity is sliding, and rig retirements in Mexico are picking up.

On the positive side, Mexico is becoming a key growth market as Pemex and private projects see additional investments. The company added 14 new contracts in Q2, which will boost the backlog and improve visibility. It has also secured long-duration work in Asia and the Middle East, supporting utilization and day rates.

The company’s cash flow surged in 6M 2025. A recent equity raise and credit expansion strengthened its liquidity. In my view, the company’s Q3 and Q4 still reflect margin strain, but the setup for 2026 looks more constructive, backed by Pemex’s renewed traction in Mexico. Although the stock’s relative valuation looks stretched, weighing all the factors, I recommend a “Buy.”

data.title: Sell Lululemon - Too Early To Buy After Another Stock Price Decline
data.description:
Lululemon Athletica, Inc. (NASDAQ:LULU) may enjoy a short-lived increase in price-per-share [PPS] as we approach the December-January Santa Claus Rally and January Effect, so [1] a “trade” or [2] an opportunity to exit your existing position at a lower loss may present itself. However, I do not believe any increase in PPS will “stick” for the long-term. For this reason, I recommend a SELL.

Some Broad Background Information

LULU promotes its “Lululemon Athletica” and “Ivivva Athletica”-branded products.

This Canadian athleisure manufacturer promotes an active and enjoyable lifestyle. Their products are priced at a premium.

Suppliers

LULU has…a group of approximately 45 vendors manufactures the products, five of which produced 56% of them, with the largest manufacturer producing 15%. …39% of Lululemon's products were manufactured in Vietnam, 14% in Cambodia, 12% in Sri Lanka, 8% in Bangladesh, and 7% in Indonesia, and the remainder in other regions.

Source: Investopedia

I wanted to take a quick look at US dollar versus [1] Vietnamese, [2] Cambodian, [3] Sri Lankan, [4] Bangladesh, and [5] Indonesian currencies. I could, also, look at the Canadian dollar, but I decided to focus on US currency.

I am not concerned about any increase or decrease in net asset values or their impact via foreign currency exchange rates in these countries. I am focused on the expenses incurred and the impact on cost of goods sold and/or cost of revenues measures.

I decided to look at the last 5 years, as follows:

For the Indonesian Rupiah:

The above illustrate a little bit of a mixed bag, but I see no horribly significant unfavorable changes over the past 5 years and with respect to foreign currency exchange rates. I would not anticipate an issue. Though US tariffs may have an impact, these matters are beyond the scope of this article and/or have been addressed by others and in recent headlines.

Some Recent Stock Price Moves

The below chart contains data captured on Monday, September 15, 2025. Before deciding if I would write this article and whether to proceed with a sell recommendation, I wanted to wait a few weeks to see if the decline in the stock price that occurred in the past repeated and/or suggested and additional pattern of declining price-per-share [PPS]. Note that the PPS is down 38.55% on that date [see below chart].

Also, note the very heavy volume for [1] the March 28 decline in PPS and [2] the June 6 decline in PPS [see above chart].

The below chart, also, contains data captured on Monday, September 15, 2025. Note that the year-to-date [YTD] PPS is down 58.20% on that date [see below chart].

Seeking Alpha Analysts Disagree with Wall Street Analysts

Seeking Alpha analysts favor LULU. Wall Street does not. The below are snips from the Seeking Alpha site. This data was captured on September 30, 2025, and follows:

The below is an Excel file I created to examine these extremely different views and recommendations for LULU:

I decided to combine [1] the Buy and Strong Buy and [2] the Sell and Strong Sell ratings. While you may disagree with my characterization, the Seeking Alpha analysts commit to a strong position with a favorable or unfavorable recommendation. Wall Street analysts are, effectively, sitting on the sidelines, with a Hold recommendation from 76.5% of them, as follows:

A Comparison with LULU Peers

With 19 Seeking Alpha analysts and 34 Wall Street analysts, LULU enjoys the highest number of both Seeking Alpha and Wall Street analyst coverage, when compared to peers, as follows:

Price return measures for LULU, relative to peers, is unfavorable. The below illustrates. LULU has the most unfavorable YTD performance, as follows:

LULU has the most unfavorable 1-year price return performance, as follows:

LULU has the most unfavorable 3-year price return performance, as follows:

LULU has the most unfavorable 5-year price return performance, as follows:

LULU has the most unfavorable 10-year price return performance, as follows:

Note, in the above, 10-year price return comparison with peers, that the 10-year global peak for LULU occurred in December 2023. A later local peak for LULU occurred in December 2024. These are seasonal peaks and relate to anticipation of favorable holiday season sales and revenues. However, the decline in PPS continued after the December Santa Claus Rally and January Effect seasonal months. There was no increase in PPS following the conclusion of the January Effect period.

Finally, I use the “maximum” feature on the Seeking Alpha platform to examine price return measures for LULU and peers. Note the highly [negatively] correlated and nearly mirror image of the price return increase for Tapestry, Inc. [TPR] and the price return decrease for LULU, as follows:

Ralph Lauren Corporation [RL] also enjoyed an increase in price return [see the light green line in the above graphic].

Continuing to use the “maximum” feature on Seeking Alpha, note the dramatic increase and leadership position that LULU enjoys, relative to peers, for their Cost of Revenues, as follows:

Continuing to use the “maximum” feature on Seeking Alpha, the below depicts and is consistent with the above chart in terms of their Gross Profit Margin where, like Cost of Revenues, from the above graphic, LULU is a relatively poor performing peer – not quite at the bottom, but second from the bottom [see the below chart, where Amer Sports, Inc. or AS is the worst performing peer, but rising quickly and showing improvement in Gross Profit Margin, whereas LULU is not rising as quickly and/or is relatively flat, showing little or no signs of improvement in Gross Profit Margin], as follows:

Continuing to use the “maximum” feature on Seeking Alpha, note the global maximum measure of operating cash flows for LULU in April 2024, followed by a dramatic decrease that is not nearly as significant for peers, as follows:

To summarize some of the above financial measures, as depicted in charts comparing LULU to peers over time, [1] LULU has performed unfavorably on price return over YTD, 1-year, 3-year, 5-year, 10-year and maximum time periods; [2] LULU did appear to enjoy short-lived, favorable price return measures for the December 2023 and 2024 Santa Claus Rally and January Effect seasonal periods, but these seasonals were followed by a significant, long-term decline; [3] the price return decline was quite dramatic for LULU following the December 2024 Christmas shopping season, while TPR and RL enjoyed favorable price returns; [4] LULU continues to enjoy the highest Cost of Revenues and the most dramatic increase in the same and is the second lowest in the peer group with respect to Gross Profit Margin, with no evidence of improvement; and [5] LULU cash flows from operations peaked in April 2024, following the Christmas shopping and gift-giving seasonal…effectively a by-product of the Santa Claus Rally and January Effect, at least on an anecdotal level, where retailers tend to enjoy seasonal peaks in cash flows a few months after the previously noted seasonals.

Summary

Each purchase and sale should be viewed independently; however, a long-term investor in LULU, having suffered a significant decline in LULU PPS might choose to buy some additional shares to average down. This, in the case of an investor feeling that the company can successfully affect a turnaround.

LULU has consistently unfavorable price return measures, when compared to peers. Price return measures did improve, seasonally, during December 2023 and December 2024 and it is possible that this pattern may repeat for a third consecutive year and in December 2025; however, there was no follow-through post-January 2024 and January 2025 and after the January Effect, so keep this in mind when making a decision to initiate, increase or decrease your position in this security. LULU Cost of Revenues and Gross Profit Margin measures are relatively unfavorable, as are cash flows from operations, though foreign supplier foreign currency exchange rates do not represent a concern that I have.

You may choose to “trade” LULU, buying now and selling in December or January and/or buying additional shares to average down if you intend to remain a long-term investor or believer in the brand and this security. However, I cannot recommend a buy and recommend a Sell for those interested in long-term investments.

data.title: GXC: For Those Seeking Broad Chinese Market Coverage
data.description:
Introduction To The SPDR S&P China ETF

The SPDR® S&P China ETF (NYSEARCA:GXC), backed by the asset management firm State Street Investment Management, is a Chinese equities-focused financial product that has been in existence since March 2007. GXC, which has garnered total assets under management [AUM] of roughly $500M, has an expense ratio of 0.59% and offers an annualized dividend yield of 2.46% at current prices (GXC distributes dividends twice a year).

What Does GXC Do?

GXC attempts to generate value for its holders “primarily” by tracking the S&P China BMI Index [SCBI]. SCBI, in turn, which is a subset of the S&P Global Broad Market Index, is set up to offer exposure to the entire investable universe of Chinese-domiciled stocks that are legally available to foreign investors. Note that we used the word “primarily” and not “entirely” because GXC isn’t a replica of SCBI; far from it. This is so because rather than covering all the +3,500 Chinese stocks that SCBI covers, GXC’s management team (SSGA Fund Management) only pursues a much smaller sample of 1,244 stocks, which, in total, are believed to closely mirror the risk and return profile of the former.

The sampling strategy that GXC follows seems like a sensible and cost-efficient route to tracking SCBI, because after all, the latter is a proxy of the entire investable universe of Chinese stocks, and one is bound to run into a few illiquid, low-volume stocks from this pool that may not necessarily be practical to pursue, given the unsavory effect large purchases could have on distorting the prices of these stocks. Besides, buying and selling +3,500 stocks just to replicate an index could result in severe transaction costs in aggregate.

Since GXC resorts to just sampling the SCBI index, it also relies on ADR [American Depositary Receipts] and GDR [Global Depositary Receipts], instruments to represent some Chinese stocks in this index. ADRs and GDRs are basically U.S.-listed certificates issued by a depositary bank that owns a specific number of shares in a Chinese company and are much easier for a foreign investor to access (rather than having to deal with the administrative, timing, and liquidity encumbrances associated with buying and selling some Chinese-listed stocks).

Having said all this, do consider that the drawback of resorting to sampling is that there’s a good chance you won’t replicate the index very well, and we can see that with GXC’s tracking errors across different durations. Tracking errors tell you how different the returns of a passive ETF are from the index it tracks. Over a 3- and 5-year basis, GXC’s tracking error has been roughly 3x worse than other ETFs, and on a 1-year basis, it is close to 2x.

What Are The Key Features of GXC's Portfolio?

Even though GXC’s tracking index looks to cover the entire investable universe of Chinese-domiciled stocks that are available for foreign investors, note that because it follows a free-float adjusted market-cap weighted policy, the greatest weights go to the largest stocks (giant and large caps account for around 83% of the portfolio). Interestingly, even though the index is open to considering stocks with a market cap of over $100M (during the stock selection process), which would open a window to owning both small and micro-caps, the eventual exposure is almost non-existent, with these buckets accounting for less than 1% in total.

From a sectoral angle, one sector (consumer discretionary) dominates by quite a huge margin, taking up almost 27% of the portfolio. The next largest sector (communication services) has a weight that is around 8% lower.

From a stylistic perspective, GXC offers a relatively nice balance between large-cap growth, hybrid, and value stocks, quite unlike a lot of other portfolios, which tend to tilt to only one stylistic basket.

For those not familiar with the main characteristics of these different styles, note that growth stocks typically see strong revenue and earnings growth rates, and returns are normally facilitated through strong price momentum. Value style stocks don’t usually see the same cadence of sales and earnings growth as growth style stocks but are priced at cheap price-to-earnings, price-to-sales, and price-to-book value multiples. These stocks usually don’t witness substantial price momentum, but they usually make up for that by being very generous on the dividend distribution front. Meanwhile, hybrid style stocks or blended stocks have traits from both the value and growth cohorts.

Who Is GXC For?

Investing in GXC would be suitable for someone looking to own a wide pool (around 1,250 stocks) of relatively cheaply valued stocks from an emerging market, which is still expected to wield plenty of clout in the global economic order, even as it continues to deepen its technological prowess, particularly in the terrain of AI.

Driven by favorable government support and their open-source culture, China’s AI investments are poised to break out over the next three years and could potentially even facilitate impressive +50% returns on investment capital [ROIC] by the end of this decade. Consider also the productivity boost that could come from an AI-infused working culture and the associated benefits on the GDP front, which could be lifted by a few percentage points.

Meanwhile, note that GXC’s portfolio of stocks is priced at a relatively reasonable price to earnings (P/E) valuation of less than 15x and a price to cash flow (P/CF) valuation of less than 10x. On a P/E basis, it works out to a 25% discount over global stocks and a 40% discount over US stocks, whereas on a P/CF basis, it works out to a 33% and 17% discount, respectively.

Also, since 27% of the portfolio comes from Chinese discretionary stocks, it’s fair to say that GXC is also well placed to cater to the spending appetite of the Chinese middle class, which crossed the 500M mark and accounts for the largest share of the global middle class.

Investors who are interested in pursuing Chinese equities should note that the entire universe is broken up into different classes (A-shares, B-shares, H-shares, P-chips, etc.), with each class having some unique trait linked to currency of denomination, listing location, incorporation location, etc. Based on the image below, we can infer that GXC would be apt for an investor looking to garner access to Chinese stocks across a whole host of share classes, but those that are not domiciled in Hong Kong or Taiwan.

What Are The Risks Associated With GXC?

While GXC is a USD-denominated product that dabbles with ADRs and GDRs of Chinese stocks, it primarily focuses on stocks that are denominated in RMB (the Chinese Yuan) and the HKD (Hong Kong Dollars) which exposes it to significant currency risk. Thus, if those two currencies were to depreciate by a huge margin against the USD [US Dollar], even if the local Chinese stocks that GXC holds do very well in local currency terms, investors could end up facing a loss when those gains are converted back into USD.

As noted earlier, GXC covers a vast pool of 1,250 Chinese stocks; Prima facie, this may give investors the impression of it being a well spread-out portfolio, but the reality is that this product is quite top-heavy and has the potential to stimulate some idiosyncratic risk (the risk of loss due to adverse conditions impacting a narrow pool of stocks), given that just two stocks account for almost a quarter of the portfolio. GXC’s top two holdings – Tencent Holdings Limited (OTCPK:TCEHY) and Alibaba Group Holding Limited (BABA) have previously found themselves in the crosshairs of the Chinese regulatory authorities and remain vulnerable to regulatory oversight, particularly given their clout in the Chinese markets.

Then, even though GXC has accumulated solid enough AUM of around half a billion dollars, the shares traded per day (only around 19,000 per day, almost 50% lower than what other ETFs see) suggest that its float shares outstanding aren't huge and also that it isn’t a hugely popular product. This makes it vulnerable to being controlled by a few market operators who may even choose to profit from quick downside movements.

What Are Some Alternatives To GXC?

Investors looking for ETF alternatives oriented around the Chinese region are spoiled for choice (Seeking Alpha’s coverage itself extends to close to three dozen Chinese ETFs), but we’ll pick two from this list. The first is the most cost competitive Chinese ETF- The Franklin FTSE China ETF (FLCH) which has an expense ratio of just 0.19% (the average expense ratio of Chinese ETFs works out to 0.66%), and the second is the most popular Chinese ETF- The KraneShares CSI China Internet ETF (KWEB) which has amassed AUM of close to $10B (the average AUM of Chinese ETFs is only around $1.25B).

FLCH, like GXC, attempts to provide broad Chinese market exposure (covering close to 1,000 stocks) for investors by tracking the FTSE China Capped Index-NR, whereas KWEB is more of a niche and focused play on Chinese internet and internet-related tech stocks. The latter tracks the CSI Overseas China Internet Index and does not cover stocks listed in China but on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange, the Nasdaq, and the NYSE.

We noted earlier how GXC is quite top-heavy, and even though its top two stocks take up a huge chunk of the portfolio, its top 10 exposure is not as pronounced as KWEB, which appears to be a very concentrated bet. KWEB is the least cost efficient of the three with an expense ratio of 0.7%, witnesses a great deal of churn every year (54%, or more than 1 in 2 stocks get turned over), has the highest risk profile (annual volatility ratio that is 700bps more than the other two) and it only pays dividends annually (although the yield is the best out of the lot).

Besides its superior cost competitiveness, FLCH also stands out for being quite stable (annual churn rate of just 9%). However, giant-cap exposure is the most out of the three, with two-thirds of the portfolio coming from that pocket.

Summary

The SPDR S&P China ETF provides access to a wide pool of cheaply valued Chinese stocks with differing share classes. While GXC is also well poised to benefit from the spending appetite of the largest middle class in the world, it also remains susceptible to idiosyncratic risks, given the strong linkage to just a couple of stocks.

This article answers these three main questions about GXC.

  • What are the main characteristics of GXC?
  • Who is GXC meant for, and what are the risks they could face?
  • How does GXC compare to other China ETFs?

data.title: Sprott: The Stellar AUM Growth Continues
data.description:
Overview

Sprott Inc. (NYSE:SII)(TSX:SII:CA) is a North American investment company that I have covered frequently, going back to 2018, where my prior articles can be found here. This article will cover the Q2 2025 results that were released in early August and the performance of the exchange-listed products ("ELP") segment during Q3 2025. The numbers for the ELP segment are collected from quarterly reports and, more recently, from the Sprott homepage at the end of each quarter.

As illustrated in the segment below, about half of the company's assets are gold-related products, about 1/4 are silver-related products, and much of the remaining 1/4 are uranium-related products.

Total assets under management ("AUM") were $40B for Sprott at the end of Q2 2025, and that number will be closer to $50B by the end of Q3 2025. That estimate is based on the growth we have seen in the ELP segment and the strong metal prices during the quarter, which will have boosted the value of the managed equities segment as well.

The ELP segment has been the largest and most important segment as long as I have covered Sprott, but its relative size has grown materially over the years. It now accounts for 85% of adjusted EBITDA, excluding the corporate segment, and 85% of AUM.

The EBITDA margin is as high as 80% in the ELP segment, compared to 30-50% in the private strategies and managed equities segment. So, the growth here has increased AUM, earnings and improved the margins over the years.

Q2 2025 Result

Total AUM grew $5B during Q2 2025, which equated to a 14% growth rate compared to Q1 2025. All the growth came from the ELP and managed equities segments, which also posted healthy growth rates for adjusted EBITDA, while the private strategies segment saw marginally lower AUM and declining adjusted EBITDA.

The relatively poor performance of the private strategies segment has been disappointing, where AUM has now been declining for 6 consecutive quarters, and adjusted EBITDA hasn't been particularly encouraging either. Management has, at least in the past, been very positive about the potential growth rate in this segment, which has not lived up to expectations.

The AUM of the managed equities segment has been climbing lately due to the strong gold and silver prices, which have also led to a healthy increase in adjusted EBITDA. With that said, the segment performed similarly to the private strategies segment, seeing outflows during Q2 and year-to-date, so the AUM growth has exclusively come from higher metal and related equity prices.

Exchange-Listed Product Segment in Q3

The exchange-listed products segment has grown in terms of AUM, adjusted EBITDA, and Sprott has also been expanding the number of products in this segment. Most newly launched products are small and will in the near term only have a marginal impact on the overall segment and the company, as 90% of segment AUM is in the 6 physical trusts.

Sprott has had a relatively high success rate with its launched and acquired products. The acquisition of Uranium Participation Corp a few years back, which is now the Sprott Physical Uranium Trust - Unit (OTCPK:SRUUF), is by far the largest success, as it has now grown to $6B in AUM.

The Sprott Silver Miners & Physical Silver ETF (SLVR) is another recent success. The ETF was launched earlier this year with $2M in AUM, which has grown in less than a year to $348M now.

This year, the segment has grown AUM by $15.4B to $41.5B at the end of Q3 2025, which is very impressive. With that said, as much as $11.6B of that growth has come from performance, but $3.8B in estimated inflows is still a very healthy number. The fact that we have seen inflows in just about all products is another encouraging fact. So, Sprott has done an excellent job with its marketing.

Conclusion

The stock price performance of Sprott has also been good this year, up 99%. It is hard to be too disappointed with that, even if the stock price has underperformed the regular precious metals mining ETFs.

The earnings yield for Sprott a few years out is in the 3-4% range, which doesn't offer much of a margin of safety in my view. It is, of course, possible that the earnings estimates are somewhat conservative a few years out, but we have seen most brokers increase their estimates lately. I would argue the estimates do at the very least reflect a gold price of $3,000/oz, which is higher than what we see for many gold mining companies today.

The growth in the exchange-listed products segment has been fantastic for Sprott, but the performance of the other segments is less impressive, even if the contribution from the other segments has a much smaller impact nowadays. I also think that too large a percentage is being spent on management compensation and other expenses, which is why the AUM growth has been more impressive than the growth in earnings, cash flows, and dividends over the years.

I have published long articles and owned Sprott in the past, but I have consistently been neutral on this stock for a few years. I do think there might come a time when I will add this back to the portfolio, but I continue to be neutral on the stock and think there are better risk-reward opportunities among precious metals mining companies. So, even if Sprott has performed well this year, it has lagged the regular precious metals mining ETFs and my precious metals equity segment substantially.

data.title: September Labor Market Data: Who Needs A Jobs Report?
data.description:
With the government shutdown now underway, the BLS’s September jobs report will be delayed, leaving markets without their usual timely read on employment conditions. But the official payroll release is not the only lens on the labor market. A range of other indicators offer valuable insights into where employment stood in September.

ADP Employment

The ADP National Employment Report is the most obvious replacement for the BLS’s jobs report. It was released yesterday on schedule, giving an update on the state of the private sector labor market. The report revealed a decline of -32,000 in ADP’s measure of US private employment, a surprise reading that was well below expectations of an increase of around 50,000. Not only did the September number come in weak, but ADP also made a major downward revision to the August job gain, flipping it from a 54,000 increase to a -3,000 decline. The revision in August and the September data were both impacted by the recently released 2024 Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages (QCEW) report from the Census Bureau, which was also the source of the BLS benchmark revisions from a month ago.

The details of the report looked pretty ugly as well. Job losses were evident across both the goods and services sectors. The largest declines were seen in leisure & hospitality (-19,000) and other services (-16,000), two sectors that had been performing well in the post-pandemic economy. The only industry that saw any significant employment gain was education & health services, adding 33,000 jobs in September. This industry's outperformance is closely aligned with BLS data, which has also been reliant on hiring strength in healthcare and social services to keep hiring numbers above zero. In terms of firm size, the weakness in small and mid-sized firm hiring is evidence of broader cyclical weakness since these firms are more sensitive to weaker economic conditions.

ADP’s wage data also offered a slight nod to weakness in the labor market through its “job changers” segment. The annual pay growth of employees who reported changing jobs slipped from 7.1% YoY in August to 6.6% YoY in September, the lowest since September 2024. Weakness in this segment of employee compensation suggests that hiring demand is weakening (albeit slowly), and there are fewer opportunities for employees to switch jobs and improve their pay situation. This is consistent with the decline in the quits rate in the JOLTS data. Earlier this week (just before the shutdown), the BLS revealed that the quits rate had fallen to 1.9% in August, which is a return to post-pandemic lows that are beneath the 2018-2019 average of 2.3%.

Job Cuts

The Challenger job cuts report released this morning is another labor market indicator we can look to for some guidance. The lesser-known report looks at announcements of job cuts of US-based employers, a more niche signal that is not as comprehensive as the ADP or BLS data. In September, US employers announced 54,064 job cuts, a notable -37% MoM drop from August and a decline of -26% YoY. For Q3 2025 as a whole, US employers announced cuts totaling 202,118, the highest Q3 total since 2020 and up 16% YoY. Overall, the longer-term trends point to an increase in layoffs over the previous year, but when looking at just September, we see a deceleration in the pace of cuts compared to months earlier in the year.

Government job cuts have been a major reason for the rise in 2025 compared to the last few years, with the YTD total for public sector cuts at 299,755. However, that was not the case in September. In fact, job cuts were pretty evenly spread across industries last month and were led only slightly by services (6,290), energy (5,807), and technology (5,639). Thus, we see that when the government is not cutting a significant number of jobs, the totals are more consistent with a gradual rise in layoffs.

A more concerning signal comes from Challenger’s tracking of hiring plans. The September 2025 total of 117,313 is well below the levels seen in the same month of prior years, largely reflecting weak seasonal hiring. Challenger reported just 100,800 seasonal hiring plans in September 2025, compared with 401,850 announced by the start of October 2024. After such a soft month, year-to-date hiring plans are now running at their lowest level since 2009.

Chicago Fed Unemployment Rate Nowcast

The Chicago Fed Labor Market Indicators report is a new release, introduced just last month, that aims to nowcast various labor market measures, including the unemployment rate, ahead of the BLS’s monthly report. For September, the Chicago Fed’s Real-Time Unemployment Rate forecast came in at 4.34%, just 2 bps above the August reading of 4.32%. The slight uptick reflected a modest rise in the layoff rate and a small dip in the hiring rate, though neither change was statistically significant. The model assigns a 54.7% probability that the unemployment rate will hold steady at 4.3% or edge up to 4.4%, suggesting that labor market conditions remain stable but with a mild upward risk to unemployment. Overall, the indicator fails to provide any evidence that there was a significant increase in joblessness in September.

Jobless Claims

Indeed, the weekly jobless claims data support the assessment that joblessness did not see a sizeable increase in September. Yes, there was a high reading of 264,000 initial claims to start the month, but that was quickly followed by a more normal reading of 232,000 in the next week and a notably low reading of 218,000 in the second-to-last week of September. We do not have the data for the final week of September, but the 4-week average covering last month is currently at 237,500, trending just above 225,250 a year ago. Continued claims for the first two weeks of the month were unchanged from the end of August at 1.926 million and also ahead of the total a year ago of 1.831 million. In summary, the jobless claims data did not see a significant move in either direction, and they continue to point to a slight loosening in the labor market.

September Labor Market Overview

Indicator September Assessment Labor Demand Labor Supply
ADP Private Employment Acceleration in job losses Weaker -
Job Cuts Normalizing layoffs, weak seasonal hiring demand Weaker -
Chicago Fed Unemployment Rate Nowcast Unchanged unemployment rate - Unchanged
Jobless Claims Unchanged trend, still weaker than a year ago - Unchanged

Taken together, the September labor market data suggest a gradual cooling without clear signs of a sharp deterioration. ADP private employment pointed to an acceleration in job losses, and Challenger data highlighted weaker seasonal hiring, both underscoring softer labor demand. At the same time, the Chicago Fed’s nowcast and jobless claims showed unemployment holding broadly steady, with no meaningful change in labor supply pressures. The overall picture is one of a labor market that is loosening, most clearly through the deterioration in firms’ demand for workers, but still largely stable compared to earlier in the year. If a jobs report were to be released tomorrow, I would expect a weak nonfarm payroll increase (or perhaps another decline) with the unemployment rate remaining stable at 4.3%.

The last two days of September labor market data, which have outlined a continued loosening in the labor market, have kept the market trading dovishly. The most significant move occurred yesterday after the ADP report posted a sharp downside surprise and caused interest rates to fall and, in turn, stocks to rise. As of this morning, the 2-year and 10-year yields are down around -5 bps each since October started. In the same period, S&P futures are up around 0.7%. With major data releases like the jobs report and CPI set to be cancelled, there may not be major macro data points to push the market off its dovish propensities, especially if the secondary data points are pointing to labor market weakness. The same could be said about the Fed, which is set to make another decision on the Fed funds rate at the end of the month.

data.title: Why AMD Could Burn Investors Chasing The Rally
data.description:
It has been half a year since I published my last article about Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) in March 2025. In the conclusion of my last article, I wrote:

In the end, it comes down to the question if we assume that AMD can actually grow at a high pace and improve its profitability in the next few years. When following these rather optimistic assumptions, the stock is certainly fairly valued or might even be extremely undervalued. However, when looking at the company’s past and highly fluctuating numbers, I don’t know if we should calculate with such high-growth assumptions and assume that AMD will be constantly profitable.

This is a rather difficult question to answer, and I personally would still be rather cautious about AMD, and I still don’t think it is the perfect investment. I still see further downside risk and especially a long-lasting bear market (which I am still expecting for the next 5 to 10 years) will also have a negative impact on the stock (even if the business can grow at a high pace).

Back then, I called AMD a double-edged investment opportunity and rated the stock as a Hold. Looking back at the last six months, a bullish rating would have been more appropriate as the stock increased 52% in the meantime and clearly outperformed the S&P 500 (SP500), which increased about 17%.

In the following article, I will look at AMD once again, and I will be much more cautious this time and see the upside potential limited. As often in my articles, we start by looking at the last quarterly results.

Second Quarter Results

On August 5, 2025, AMD reported second quarter results and while earnings per share were in-line with expectations, revenue did beat expectations. Net revenue increased from $5,835 million in Q2/24 to $7,685 million in Q2/25 – resulting in 31.7% year-over-year growth for the top line. But while revenue is growing at a high pace, operating income switched from a positive amount of $269 million in the same quarter last year to an operating loss of $134 million in this quarter. The main reasons were the increased cost of sales, which jumped 59% year-over-year.

Diluted earnings per share increased from $0.16 in Q2/24 to $0.54 in Q2/25 while diluted, non-GAAP earnings per share declined from $0.69 in the same quarter last year to $0.48 in this quarter. Finally, free cash flow increased from $439 million in the same quarter last year to $1,180 million in this quarter.

AMD is reporting in three different segments:

  • Data Center: Revenue increased from $2,834 million in Q2/24 to $3,240 million in Q2/25 – resulting in 14.3% year-over-year growth. Operating income switched from an operating income of $743 million in the same quarter last year to an operating loss of $155 million in this quarter.
  • Client & Gaming: This is currently the most important segment for AMD as it is reporting the highest growth rates and the biggest part of total revenue. In Q2/25, revenue increased from $2,140 million in the same quarter last year to $3,621 million in this quarter – resulting in 69.2% year-over-year growth. Operating income increased 360% from $166 million in Q2/24 to $767 million in Q2/25.
  • Embedded Segment: Revenue declined from $861 million in Q2/24 to $824 million in Q2/25 – resulting in 4.3% decline year-over-year. Operating income declined 20.3% year-over-year to $275 million in Q2/25.

Still Expensive

Before we look at the growth potential in the years to come and the question of whether AMD can play a more important role when it comes to revenue generated from data centers and AI, we look at the price-earnings ratio and price-free-cash-flow ratio again. The following chart's significance lies in its presentation of two separate elements.

First, it is showing us once again that AMD was a rather erratic business in the past. We see valuation multiples around 1,000 on several occasions which is indicating huge fluctuations for earnings per share as well as free cash flow.

Second, the chart is also showing that AMD is also trading for high valuation multiples right now. AMD is trading for 65 times free cash flow and 93 times earnings at this point and to justify such high valuation multiples a business certainly has to grow with a high pace.

Growth Potential: Data Center

As mentioned above, AMD has to grow with a high pace in the years to come to justify current valuation multiples and when talking about drivers of growth, most analysts are mentioning revenue from data center and AI, which is growing with a high pace. However, as mentioned above, AMD grew its data center revenue in the double digits, but “only” 14% year-over-year. In Q1/25, revenue even declined 4.8% year-over-year and compared to previous quarters growth slowed down from extremely high growth rates (revenue increased 69% in Q4/24 and 122% in Q3/24).

Especially when comparing AMD to Nvidia (NVDA), we see AMD clearly struggling as Nvidia is still reporting extremely high growth rates for its Data Center business. Nvidia grew its data center revenue from $26,272 million in Q2/25 to $41,096 million in Q2/25.

This is not only resulting in a much higher growth rate (56.4% year-over-year growth), but Nvidia is generating more than 10 times the revenue AMD is generating from Data Centers right now. The following chart about generative AI market shares also makes this obvious – Nvidia is dominating this market with a share of 92% in 2024 and AMD only being able to grab a market share of 4.0% (the other companies are not even worth mentioning here).

In my last article about NVIDIA, I wrote about the risk that Nvidia might not be able to hold on to its high market shares in the years to come. And I still think this is a risk for Nvidia we should keep in mind. However, when looking at the numbers AMD is reporting, I also have to admit that AMD does not seem like much of a threat to Nvidia. Especially as the market share of Nvidia seems to expand again and was 94% in the second quarter.

Nevertheless, analysts are very optimistic for AMD’s growth potential in the years to come. When looking at earnings revision trends, we see estimates lowered again in the last few quarters. Especially estimates for fiscal 2025 and fiscal 2026 were lowered in the last few quarters. At the end of December 2024, analysts estimated earnings per share for fiscal 2025 to be $5.09 and for fiscal 2026 to be $7.04. Now these estimates have been lowered to $3.90 and $6.01.

Despite analysts getting a little more pessimistic, earnings per share are still expected to grow with a high pace in the next few years. Between fiscal 2024 and fiscal 2029, earnings per share are expected to grow with a CAGR of 26.12%, which are certainly high growth expectations (even higher expectations than for NVIDIA, which is expected to grow its bottom line with a CAGR of 24.43% in the next five years).

When looking at past growth rates for AMD, we should be very cautious about such high growth rates. In the last five years, revenue was growing with a CAGR of 30.82% and this would certainly be high enough, but the question remains if AMD can keep up these growth rates.

This is especially important when taking into account a risk I already mentioned in my last Nvidia article. Right now, the major tech companies - especially Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG), Meta Platforms (META) and Microsoft (MSFT) – are spending heavily on AI infrastructure and are purchasing hardware (especially from Nvidia). Up until now these companies are growing with a high pace but what will happen when the huge AI investments can’t be turned into profits. There are certainly reports about many companies abandoning AI projects. S&P Global is writing:

The percentage of companies abandoning the majority of their AI initiatives before they reach production has surged from 17% to 42% year over year, with organizations on average reporting that 46% of projects are scrapped between proof of concept and broad adoption. This marks a significant departure from the previous downward trend, highlighting the difficulties many organizations face in rapidly deploying AI projects.

This is a risk we certainly must take into account. These companies investing heavily in AI capacities also are listed companies and therefore must answer to the shareholders. As long as shareholders believe these investments are making sense, it won’t be a problem. But if sentiment might change, questions about declining free cash flow might arise and such a change in sentiment will affect Nvidia much more than AMD as the company mostly purchase chips from Nvidia. But the question remains whether AMD will be able to grow close to 30% annually like analysts are expecting.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

And AMD certainly needs such high growth rates for the stock price to be justified. I already wrote above that the high valuation multiples indicate that the stock is rather expensive and high growth rates are necessary. This can be backed up by using a discount cash flow calculation.

In the past, I almost always used a discount rate of 10% as this is the annual return I like to achieve at least. However, in case of AMD let’s calculate a WACC to determine if such a metric is reasonable here and. – spoiler – it is hard to justify a 10% discount rate for AMD. When taking the last reported debt numbers, the current equity value, the beta and cost of capital according to recent filing and use the10-year treasury yield as risk free rate and also use a market risk premium of 5%, we get a WACC of 14.5% for AMD. Even when lowering the market risk premium to only 4%, the WACC for AMD is still 12.5% - making a discount rate of 10% hard to justify in discount cash flow calculation.

Hence, let’s calculate with the following assumptions. As basis, we use the free cash flow of the last four quarters, which was $4,043 million. Additionally, we calculate with a discount rate of 12.5% (see above) and the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding was 1,630 million. For the next ten years we assume 26% growth (in line with analysts’ assumptions) followed by 6% growth till perpetuity. Using these assumptions, we get an intrinsic value $138.45 for AMD and the stock therefore seems undervalued and is not a good investment.

We should also keep in mind that these assumptions are very optimistic. Not only are we assuming 6% growth till perpetuity (which is above the recommended terminal growth rate), we also assume 26% growth for the next ten years and considering AMD’s past performance and the challenges for the economy right now, we might also be too optimistic. And we could also use a WACC of 14.5% (like in my original WACC calculation) and this leads to an intrinsic value of only $98.61.

Conclusion

In my opinion, AMD is not a good investment at this point, and especially the 50% stock price increase since my last article made the stock overvalued. And when looking at the results, I am neither seeing the consistency Nvidia has in its results, nor does it seem likely that AMD will take major market shares from Nvidia in the next few quarters.

Investors seem to be very optimistic and drove the stock price to a level, which is hard to justify. Around $100, the stock price could rather be justified when assuming a great performance for AMD in the years to come, but now the company has to perform at such an exceptional level that I won’t take this bet. In my opinion, AMD is a Sell at this point, and while I don’t want to bet against the company and short it, investors might consider trimming the position. And in my opinion, analysts with a median price target of $190 are too optimistic.

data.title: EVG: Short Duration Losing Appeal And Discount Remains Tight
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Eaton Vance Short Duration Diversified Income Fund (NYSE:EVG) is a closed-end fund that provides investors with exposure to a diversified portfolio of fixed-income instruments. As the fund's name would imply, they more specifically focus on those securities that are considered short-duration (i.e., less interest rate sensitive). That makes the fund less interest rate sensitive in terms of underlying prices, which can be more appealing in a rising rate environment. However, when the Fed is cutting its target rate, that can also mean less income generation as yields decline.

On the other hand, EVG is leveraged and utilizes borrowings through a credit agreement based on the secured overnight financing rate ("SOFR"), similar to a large portion of its underlying portfolio. So there is some offset that the fund will see from its borrowing costs declining. Even further, the fund employs futures and swap contracts, which can help hedge against rate changes as well.

Even with a weaker relative environment for the fund's underlying portfolio, in terms of its attractiveness relative to longer-duration assets currently in a rate-cutting environment, what I focus on more is the valuation. The discount has continued to narrow, and that was the same problem we saw previously, with the fund's discount in the low single digits now shifting to near parity with the NAV per share.

EVG Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 0.90
  • Discount/Premium: -026%
  • Distribution Yield: 7.92%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.54%
  • Leverage: 29.3%
  • Managed Assets: $186.7 million
  • Structure: Perpetual

EVG is a relatively simple fund; it "seeks to provide a high level of current income." The secondary objective is to "also seek capital appreciation to the extent consistent with its primary goal of high income."

In an attempt to achieve this, their investment policy is rather flexible as well. The fund "will invest at least 25% of its net assets in each of the following three investment categories: [i] senior, secured floating rate loans made to corporate and other business entities, which are typically rated below investment grade; [ii] bank deposits denominated in foreign currencies, debt obligations of foreign governmental and corporate issuers, including emerging market issuers, which are denominated in foreign currencies or U.S. dollars, and positions in foreign currencies; and [iii] mortgage-backed securities that are issued, backed, or otherwise guaranteed by the U.S. Government or its agencies or instrumentalities or that are issued by private issuers."

The fund is leveraged through both borrowings and derivatives, which can amplify moves in either direction. Leverage through borrowings also adds further interest expenses to the fund, which would push the fund's total expense ratio to 3.07% as of its last reporting. These borrowing costs should ease as the Fed continues to cut its benchmark rate.

Performance - Discount Tightens

It has been quite some time since we've covered the fund, going back to just over two years ago on Sept. 26, 2023. The fund is a multi-sector bond fund, but it is primarily tilted toward mortgage-backed securities and senior loans.

That can make it more comparable to the Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN) and iShares MBS ETF (MBB) as being more similar. However, some smaller exposure to high-yield investments can make the iShares iBoxx High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (HYG) also a general barometer for the fund. I've also included iShares iBoxx Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD) for good measure.

Either way, no matter what ETF you may want to use as a barometer here, EVG has come out on top by a fairly wide margin. Only HYG came close on a total share price and total NAV return basis.

As we can see that the fund's total share price returns have outpaced the fund's total NAV returns in this period, we know that the fund would have seen its narrow discount previously narrow even further. In fact, this fund has been flirting with a premium more recently. We can see that over the longer term, this is much narrower than the fund's average.

Of course, when we were in a zero-rate or near-zero environment, the fund would generally be less appealing as a short-duration portfolio. As rates started to climb aggressively, it would have made EVG look more appealing.

Still, while the higher rate environment would have been a positive to help reduce the discount, that doesn't appear to have been the ultimate driving factor to help push the fund to a premium. Instead, it was tinkering with the distribution that they were paying out.

Distribution Reduction But More Sustainable

Even before rates were rising, the fund boosted its payout significantly to investors to reduce the discount. We've seen this happen many times in CEFs, as these funds are often targeted by activists; this is one of the primary go-to strategies. Funds can essentially payout whatever they'd like until their NAV hits $0.

For this fund, that is when they implemented a significantly higher payout, but one that would vary each month based on what was originally 10% of NAV. It was then reduced to 8.5%, but now it has been reduced again to a target closer to 8% at the start of this year. The latest NAV rate comes in at 7.90%—and due to only a slim discount, the market rate comes in similar at 7.92%.

While a managed distribution policy can provide appeal, and it made sense to raise the payout during a higher rate environment, this was driven by wanting to get investors on board to approve the manager change when Morgan Stanley (MG) bought Eaton Vance. The 10% level was unsustainable for the fund, and that's why we would have seen the continual decline after switching to that level of payout.

When switching to 8.5% and now 8%, it becomes much more manageable. In fact, from the start of this year to now, the trend has been slightly higher. January's payout was $0.0735, and last month it was $0.0747.

With a lower payout from the initial huge boost in payout, we've seen a trend of continually better coverage. We even started to see higher NII into 2023/2024 as the Fed was raising its benchmark rate aggressively. With the latest semi-annual report, we are starting to see the declines happen now as the Fed started cutting in late 2024.

We had a more recent cut in September, and that will likely once again see some lower income generation and therefore, lower NII in the fund. While the fund will see its borrowing costs also decline, it isn't enough to offset all the declines from its underlying portfolio.

Therefore, the fund will continue to require capital gains if it can achieve those, to help cover the shortfall in the payout. If they can do that, then the fund's NAV can remain flat, and we could consider it "covering" its payout.

EVG's Portfolio

Given that EVG is focused on short duration, it isn't too surprising to see the fund's leverage-adjusted duration come in at 2.6 years. They are going to achieve that primarily thanks to their positioning in floating-rate securities, such as senior loans and collateralized loan obligations. However, some of their MBS and CMBS will also come with floating rate exposure.

High-yield exposure can also keep the fund relatively lower in terms of interest rate sensitivity, too, as it comes with relatively lower maturities and higher yields. Overall, the fund is tilted more toward the lower credit quality spectrum, with below investment grade exposure comprising 53.9% of the fund and another 3.6% unrated.

That can increase credit risks for the fund, but similar to how they can hedge rates through derivatives, they can also hedge on this front with credit default swaps.

Further, the fund offers significant diversification, with CEFConnect listing over 600 holdings. Within the underlying holdings, there can be even more exposure. With each MBS and CLO, and other pooled securities the fund holds, it has hundreds or thousands of packaged loans within those as well. Meaning, in general, the fund may not produce much in terms of alpha since it is so diversified (beyond what it can deliver with leverage potentially), but it also tends to mean that the whole economy will have to go sideways for this fund to be significantly impacted. In other words, there isn't a single holding that can have too much sway on the fund's overall performance.

Conclusion

EVG provides a multi-sector bond fund approach with a focus on providing short duration, with the fund's leverage-adjusted duration; at 2.6 years, they are delivering just that. However, a lower short-rate environment also means the fund's income generation can be negatively impacted. In reflecting that, it appears they have reduced the managed payout to come in at 8% of NAV. Holding some short-duration fund such as EVG isn't necessarily a negative over the long term. That said, where I continue to remain neutral more is due to the fund's narrow discount.

data.title: Hama Intelligence Readies U.S. IPO Plan
data.description:
Hama Is Growing, But Valuation Is High

Hama Intelligence Limited (HAMA) has filed to raise $6.6 million in an IPO of its Class A ordinary shares, according to SEC F-1/A registration information.

HAMA provides various corporate and business advisory services in Hong Kong and Singapore.

The company is growing revenue from a tiny base but has numerous risks and extreme valuation assumptions for the IPO.

My outlook on the HAMA IPO is to sell (avoid it).

What Does Hama Do?

Singapore-based Hama Intelligence Limited was founded to develop various business financial and advisory service offerings for small and medium-sized firms.

Management is led by Chief Executive Officer Mr. Wai Ting Cheung, who has been with the firm since 2025 and was previously director and responsible officer of Seeds International Asset Management.

The company's primary offerings include:

  • One-stop corporate services.

  • Business financial consulting.

  • Accounting.

  • Internal controls.

  • Tax and compliance advisory.

  • Investor relationship management.

HAMA has booked a fair market value investment of approximately $185,000 in debt as of February 28, 2025, from investor Mr. Wing Sum Ho.

The company's revenue contribution by segment and timing is shown in the pie charts below, with Business & Financial Consulting generating the most revenue by segment:

Employee & Compensation expenses as a percentage of total revenue have fallen as revenues have risen, as the figures below indicate:

Expenses vs. Revenue

(Source: SEC).

The Employee & Compensation efficiency multiple, i.e., how many dollars of additional revenue are produced from each dollar of Employee & Compensation expense, was 2.2x in the most recent reporting period. (Source: SEC).

What Is Hama's Market?

According to a 2025 market research report by Mordor Intelligence, the market in Hong Kong for management consulting services is approximately $1 billion in 2025 and is expected to reach $1.3 billion by 2030.

This represents a projected CAGR of around 6% from 2026 to 2030, a moderate expected growth rate.

The primary factors driving this expected growth are cross-border complexities between Hong Kong and mainland China and a growing strategic significance of the Greater Bay Area region.

Also, some drivers of expected growth of the market in the greater Hong Kong area are described in the table below:

The company faces a fragmented and highly competitive market with low barriers to entry and competitors include other corporate consultancy firms and professional service firms of various sizes and degrees of specialization.

The firm pursues clients from among small and medium-sized businesses for its full range of corporate service offerings.

HAMA targets businesses located in Hong Kong and throughout the Greater Bay Area region of Guangzhou-Shenzhen-Hong Kong-Macau-Zhuhai.

I've prepared a thumbnail SWOT analysis for HAMA:

Strengths

  • Broad service offerings and ability to cross-sell, diverse client base, and lower regulatory risk.

Weaknesses

  • No long-term customer contracts, low switching costs, reliance on a non-US auditor.

Opportunities

  • Geographic expansion in Southeast Asia, growing regulatory complexity, and leveraging technology and automation (Generative AI) to scale more efficiently.

Threats

  • Chinese regulatory and political risks and wide competition with low barriers to entry.

Hama's Recent Financial Results

The firm's recent financial performance is summarized as shown below:

  • Increasing topline revenue from a small base.

  • Higher gross profit and gross margin.

  • Growing operating profit and cash flow from operations.

Hama's IPO Plan

HAMA intends to sell 1.1 million shares of Class A ordinary shares at a proposed midpoint price of $6.00 per share for gross proceeds of approximately $6.6 million, not including the sale of customary underwriter options.

No existing or new shareholders have indicated an interest in acquiring additional shares of the IPO, as described in the prospectus.

Class A stockholders will receive one vote per share.

Class B votes will have twenty (20) votes per share and the company will be a 'controlled company' immediately post-IPO.

Company, shareholder and executive lockups will be for the typical 180-day period following the effective date of the prospectus.

Dilution in net tangible book value per share for investors in the IPO is expected to be $5.71, which is within the typical range for a company at IPO.

The company's enterprise value at IPO (assuming no underwriter options) will approximate $115 million.

The float-to-outstanding-shares ratio (excluding underwriter options) will be approximately 5.47%, so the stock will be a 'low float' stock subject to higher volatility in open market trading.

According to HAMA's most recent regulatory filing, it plans to use the net proceeds as follows:

approximately 30% for business development and marketing;

approximately 30% for expanding our local operations and setting up overseas branches; and approximately 40% for general administration and working capital.

(Source: SEC).

The firm's presentation of the company roadshow isn't available.

Pertaining to potential legal exposure, leadership says the company is not currently the target of legal actions that would have a material adverse effect on its financial condition or operations.

Listed underwriters of the IPO are Pacific Century Securities

Hama Is Growing And Profitable But Faces Risks

Hama is seeking US public capital market funding before the US market becomes more difficult for small Asian IPOs.

Hama's financials have shown growing topline revenue from a small base, increased gross profit and gross margin and higher operating profit and cash flow from operations.

Free cash flow for the twelve months ended February 28, 2025, was approximately $1.0 million.

Employee & Compensation expenses as a percentage of total revenue fell as revenue grew and its Employee & Compensation efficiency multiple was 2.2x in the most recent fiscal year.

The firm currently plans to pay no dividends in the foreseeable future and will likely retain any future earnings for reinvestment in its growth plans and working capital needs.

Hama's recent capital spending history shows it has spent lightly on capital expenditures as a percentage of its operating cash flow.

The firm's growth strategy includes expanding into other Southeast Asian markets, pursuing selective acquisitions, improving operating leverage through Gen AI tools and other automations, and cross-selling services to clients.

The market opportunity for providing corporate advisory and business services is large and expected to grow at only a moderate rate of growth and features intense competition from market players of all sizes.

Risks to the company's outlook as a public firm include its 'foreign private issuer' and 'emerging growth company' status, which enables management to divulge less information to shareholders.

Many of these types of Asian companies have performed unpredictably in open market trading, with most providing poor financial returns to investors.

Nasdaq is also in the process of increasing minimum IPO size and implementing other hurdles that will likely sharply reduce these small-cap IPOs.

The firm has geographic concentration risks due to its focus on Hong Kong and does not have long-term binding contracts with its clients, leading to the risk of client switching or loss.

As to valuation, HAMA is seeking an EV/Revenue multiple of 63x and an EV/EBITDA multiple of 92x, both of which are extreme and unwarranted.

Also, the table below shows a comparison of US publicly held business and consumer service companies compared to HAMA, as compiled by noted valuation expert Dr. Aswath Damodaran, providing further evidence of extremely high valuation expectations:
