data.title: AbCellera: Shares Have Momentum, But Does The Pipeline? It's Too Early To Tell
data.description:
Investment Overview

I have been quite critical of AbCellera's (NASDAQ:ABCL) business in notes for Seeking Alpha in the past - awarding its stock a sell rating in March 2023, followed by three hold ratings in December 2023, May 2024, and March this year, so it is only fair to lavish some praise on the company given its share price is up >135% since my last hold call - although it remains down nearly 20% since my original sell call.

AbCellera is a Vancouver, British Columbia based biotech company that, according to its Q2 2025 quarterly report / 10Q filing is focused on "the development of antibody-based drugs", and is "committed to improving discovery and development". In my last post I discussed how:

AbCellera was able to develop 2 COVID anti-virals alongside partner Eli Lilly (LLY) in bamlanivimab and bebtelovimab, which helped it post revenues of $233m in 2020, $375.2m in 2021, and $485.4m in 2022, before the drug was withdrawn from the market as it was no longer effective against newer strains of COVID.

Since then, it has been slim pickings for AbCellera, revenues-wise - $38m in 2023, and $28.8m in 2024, while combined operating income of ~$225m in 2021/22, has become a combined net loss of >$(520m) in 2023/24.

The company's IPO in December 2020 raised ~$483m, at $20 per share, and stock currently trades at $6.5 per share, so performance has been underwhelming, however shares traded <$1 in May this year, and are up >195% on a six-month basis.

I had been critical of AbCellera's business model - working with pharma partners and attempting to discover the next bamlanivimab and bebtelovimab, in exchange for milestone payments and royalties - in previous notes, suggesting that with such a model, AbCellera takes on the all of the risks of early stage drug discovery - the proverbial search for a needle in a haystack - while allowing partners to reap most of the reward - potentially, billions of dollars of commercial revenues, while AbCellera earns perhaps a few hundred million in milestones, plus a single digit percentage royalty stream.

To its credit, management appears to have rapidly pivoted its business to in-house drug development (if you have the tools and believe in them, why not build wholly owned programs with them?), as we can see in the slide below (taken from a recent corporate presentation):

AbCellera - Internal Pipeline Overview - Phase 1 Data Catalysts In 2026

AbCellera expects to share Phase 1 data from studies of both of its lead candidates, ABCL635, and ABCL575 in 2026.

ABCL635 is described as "a potential first-in-class antibody for the non-hormonal treatment of vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes)", that targets the neurokonin 3 receptor, which is a G protein-coupled receptor ("GPCR"), and targets a disease with an addressable market of ~40m women of menopausal age in the US, 30% of whom experience moderate-to-severe VMS, AbCellera's research suggests.

ABCL635 is dosed monthly, and subcutaneously, which AbCellera hopes will be a competitive advantage over current standards of care, the main one being menopause hormone therapy ("MHT"). AbCellera says that ~12% of women are contraindicated for MHT, however, and cites research suggesting 57% of women are against using MHT.

AbCellera also points to the fact that Astellas Pharma's Veozah (fezolinetant) and Bayer's Lynkuet (elinzanetant) - the first having been approved in the US in 2023 to treat hot flashes, and the second under regulatory review - are both small molecule NK3R antagonists, as evidence its drug can be successful, although presumably these two drugs are also its direct rivals for market share, if approval is secured one day.

As mentioned, ABCL635 is a monthly injectable as opposed to a daily oral drug like Veozah and Lynkuet, and an antibody-based therapeutic as opposed to a small molecule, and not therefore associated with liver toxicity. Stock has been climbing ever since AbCellera announced it had obtained authorisation from Health Canada to initiate a Phase 1 study of the drug.

On May 30th this year, also doubtless contributing to recent gains, AbCellera announced that it had dosed a first patient in a Phase 1 study of its second internal candidate, ABCL-575, an OX40L antagonist - a similar mechanism of action ("MoA") as a candidate being developed by Pharma giant Sanofi (SNY), amlitelimab, which recently aced a Phase 3 study in atopic dermatitis ("AD"), and Amgen's (AMGN) rocatilimab, also in Phase 3 studies in AD. The advantage of ABCL-575 appears to be its potential once-every six-month dosing regime.

Regarding ABCL635, AbCellera's CEO Carl Hansen told analysts on its Q2 earnings call:

we believe the main scientific risk for ABCL635 is whether or not we can achieve sufficient target engagement. We expect this will be addressed through biomarker and proof-of-concept studies that are part of our Phase I design. As previously stated, we expect initial safety and efficacy data from this trial in mid-2026.

And regarding, ABCL-575 the CEO commented:

We recently presented preclinical data, which demonstrates it has potent functional activity in vitro that is in line with Amlitelimab as measured by cytokine responses across a variety of cytokines, including IL-13, IL-5 and IL-9.

As a reminder, ABCL575 is engineered with half-life extension to support less frequent dosing. Based on PK data we have obtained from studies in FcRn humanized mice, we predict a human half-life of approximately 67 days.

AbCellera is also hoping to secure an Investigational New Drug ("IND") approval (which allows the company to initiate a Phase 1 study) for a third candidate, ABCL688, an ion channel- or GPCR-targeted antibody development candidate focused on autoimmune diseases. The IND submission has been promised for mid-2026.

Royalty & Partnered Programs

Given AbCellera has spent the past ten years perfecting its drug development technology platform, and reported a cash position of $553m as of the end of Q2, it makes sense to use it at full capacity, and the company still has plenty of partnered programs in development.

AbCellera says it has 96 partner initiated programs with "downstream participation", with 16 molecules in the clinic, with a mean royalty rate of 3.2%, and 37 "understood to be progressing" (source: investor presentation). AbCellera shares the below overview of programs, which in my view is not especially compelling, although there does appear to be three oncology programs in the clinic and active, alongside a single neurosicence candidate:

Analysis - Stock Has Momentum, But Does Company?

AbCellera's market cap today stands at $1.78bn, which seems fairly high for a biotech with just two Phase 1 stage drug candidates, and a handful of partnered assets in the clinic.

Stock has been soaring in value, however - up >25% in the last week, although it is difficult to find specific reasons why. The Sanofi amlitelimab data may have helped boost optimism around the 575 program, and the market may be celebrating the fact that AbCellera is now entering the clinic with two assets of its own, both of which have potentially unique advantages over the competition.

With that said, however, AbCellera still burns through cash fairly rapidly - net loss in 1H26 was $(148.35m) - and it is years behind its competition in both the fields of hot flashes and atopic dermatitis - its assets have proven nothing in the clinic as yet.

Still, if we consider the long-term, the market clearly seems to believe AbCellera can play the long game and win. It's not always easy for a layman to look at a biotech's preclinical work and predict a path to success for a drug candidate, or to evaluate the promise of one manufacturing plant or technology platform over another, but clearly, AbCellera's efforts have some support. The likes of VC firms Baker Brothers, Baillie Gifford and Blackrock are all invested in AbCellera stock.

It can certainly help a biotech to have influential contacts in the world of finance, and in AbCellera's case, it may also help to have patient ones, because, two Phase 1 data readouts and an IND submission aside, there are few investment highlights / catalysts expected this year, or next.

Concluding Thoughts - Does AbCellera Stock Warrant An Upgrade, or Downgrade From Current Hold Rating?

This is tricky question to answer because, as mentioned, by most traditional measures its hard to justify AbCellera's current valuation - a biotech with two Phase 1 stage assets whose MoAs are similar to drugs close to being approved would not normally warrant a market cap valuation in the triple-digit-millions, in my experience, let alone in the billions.

Clearly, however, the market is showing a lot of faith in AbCellera, and if we home in on the drug candidates, perhaps their unique competitive advantages - monthly subcutaneous dosing, potential for better safety, longer half-lives, large market opportunities - are more compelling than they seem.

I think it's interesting to note that AbCellera states in its presentation that, in relation to ABCL-635, that:

Increasing use of GLP-1 agonists is significantly increasing the autoinjector-experienced population

According to an abstract published to Nature.com

The glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor, known as GLP-1R, is a vital component of the G protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) family and is found primarily on the surfaces of various cell types within the human body

The GLP-1 drug class has taken the pharmaceutical world by storm over the past few years, with the advent of Novo Nordisk's (NVO) and Eli Lilly's (LLY) "miracle" weight loss and type 2 diabetes drugs semaglutide and tirzepatide, or Wegovy / Ozempic, and Zepbound / Mounjaro, which have quickly become the second and third best-selling prescription drugs in circulation, both earning >$8bn of revenues in the last quarter alone.

By playing in the GCPR space, AbCellera is potentially lining up to one day play in the GLP-1 space, which could result in its developing new drug candidates with massive market potential. With that said, the likes of Septerna (SEPN), Terns Pharma (TERN), and Altimmune (ALT) are all in the GLP-1 space, and none enjoy a >$1bn market cap, although Pfizer (PFE) did agree to acquire GLP-1 developed Metsera in September, in a deal worth an initial $4.9bn.

At this moment in time, I can't upgrade AbCellera stock to a "Buy" because objectively speaking, at this time if I apply the usual risk factors - length of time to any potential drug approval, phase of development, financial strength, track record - they suggest AbCellera stock is significantly overvalued.

Nevertheless, my interest is piqued by the recent run-up in the share price and the projects the company may work on in the future, and what its drug discovery engine may be capable of in the future - AbCellera is already talking up a fourth drug candidate it expects to unveil soon - could it be a GLP-1 receptor agonist?

It doesn't necessarily have to be, to be considered a very valuable drug - in fact, the OX40 targeting ABCL-575 candidate would be a multi-billion selling asset if it showed itself to be superior to current AD drugs, such as Sanofi's >$14bn per annum selling Dupixent.

There is definitely hope for AbCellera's future, then, but in my view, I can't see how the two upcoming Phase 1 data readouts could be compelling enough to send the company's market cap any higher, given the early stage of development, so am on the side-lines until there is more concrete evidence that the company has a best-in-class asset in its stable.
